header.name: Oliver Kaikane Gate
header.tagline: Senior full-stack engineer (React/TypeScript, Python). 7+ years across data platforms and consumer products. Building with LLMs and going deep on agentic systems.

paragraphs: Curious, restless, and interested in how things work — whether that's a supply chain, a user's mental model, or how an LLM decides to call a tool.

Came to engineering from analytical philosophy nearly 8 years ago, which still shapes how I approach problems: question the framing first, then build. I've shipped across multi-tenant SaaS, data platforms, consumer apps, and most recently sustainability tech, with a track record of going from junior IC to leading a team.

Right now I'm spending most of my time building with LLMs and going deep on agentic systems — what good orchestration looks like, where the security gaps sit, how harnesses should be designed for the next few years of this. Three side projects in flight; daily Claude Code user; gradually building my own agent and telemetry setup.
Outside of all that: food, cooking, travel, books, chess, and running.

experienceOverview: Full-stack engineer with 7+ years shipping production systems for sustainability impact ventures and financial services. Comfortable across the stack with React and TypeScript on the frontend and Python and Node on the backend; deepest in dashboards, data platforms, and multi-tenant SaaS. Progressed from junior IC to leading a team of five, with experience running external consulting engagements end-to-end. Currently building with LLMs across multiple side projects and training in agentic systems, prompt security (OWASP AI Top 10), and agent orchestration. Looking for roles where full-stack depth meets AI-native product thinking.

summary.honest: Sustainability impact venture co-founded by WWF and BCG, providing supply chain traceability for global brands. Standardised messy first-three-miles farm data into EPCIS, then surfaced it through dashboards used by Nespresso, Austral Fisheries, and internal teams to validate ethical sourcing claims and farmer payments.
summary.pessimistic: Sustainability impact venture co-founded by WWF and BCG. The work was real and the brands were real; the underlying challenge — wrangling first-three-miles farm data into something a dashboard could honestly defend — was harder and slower than any of us would have liked to admit.
summary.absurd: A sustainability impact venture co-founded by WWF and BCG, which is a sentence I still can't say without picturing pandas in suits. The job, more or less, was to convince messy farm data to behave like a respectable EPCIS citizen, then put it on a screen so coffee could be morally cleared for landing.

text.honest: Sole frontend engineer on the main analytics dashboard — built and maintained the entire system end-to-end. Plotly Dash for the analytical core; React and Next.js for the consumer-facing experiences.
text.pessimistic: Sole frontend engineer on the main analytics dashboard. End-to-end ownership is great when shipping fast and lonely when something subtle breaks at 11pm — there were stretches where I was the only person who knew how a particular Plotly Dash callback worked, and that's a bus-factor I'd inherit again only if I had to.
text.absurd: Sole frontend engineer on the main analytics dashboard, which means I was simultaneously the team lead, the team, and the team's only critic. Plotly Dash for the analytical core; React and Next.js for the bits humans were meant to enjoy. There is a Slack channel somewhere named after one of my callbacks.

text.honest: Built the traceability and payments validation system used by Nespresso to verify coffee sourcing across Rainforest Alliance and their internal AAA premium-farmer program — including checks that base prices tracked the London robusta price index and that farmer payments matched contracted standards.
text.pessimistic: Built the traceability and payments validation system Nespresso adopted for Rainforest Alliance and their AAA program. Some of the price-tracking edge cases against the London robusta index took more iteration than I'd budgeted for, and the rollout had a rougher first quarter than I'd have liked before settling into something I'd actually defend.
text.absurd: Built a traceability and payments validation system that Nespresso uses to verify coffee sourcing across Rainforest Alliance and their AAA premium-farmer program. I have personally never met a coffee bean I didn't suspect of fraud. The London robusta price index and I are now on a first-name basis; we exchange thoughts on volatility.

text.honest: Surfaced supply chain anomalies — infiltration, exfiltration, breaches of farm productivity capacity, coffee from untraced origins — through graph-based analysis powered by Kuzu.
text.pessimistic: Surfaced supply chain anomalies — infiltration, exfiltration, untraced origins — via graph analysis on Kuzu. Real catches, real value, but Kuzu was early and the queries that ran cleanly in dev sometimes did not run cleanly in production, which I learned about in the usual way.
text.absurd: Surfaced supply chain anomalies — infiltration, exfiltration, breaches of farm productivity capacity, coffee from untraced origins — via graph analysis on Kuzu. Most of my professional life is now spent asking nodes uncomfortable questions about where they came from and who they've been talking to.

text.honest: Worked with Austral Fisheries to validate the legal provenance of toothfish and prawn catches.
text.pessimistic: Worked with Austral Fisheries on legal provenance for toothfish and prawn catches. The dataset was unforgiving and the domain expertise lived three time-zones away, which made every iteration cycle longer than the code suggested.
text.absurd: Worked with Austral Fisheries on the legal provenance of toothfish and prawn catches. I now know more about the personal life of a toothfish than any human should reasonably know, and nothing has prepared me for the day someone at a dinner party asks what I do.

text.honest: Re-architected supply chain onboarding in Dagster: replaced bespoke per-cluster orchestrations with a YAML-configured, data-contract-driven ingestion layer. Turned onboarding from a custom engineering effort per supply chain into a configuration task.
text.pessimistic: Re-architected supply chain onboarding in Dagster, replacing bespoke per-cluster orchestrations with YAML-configured, data-contract-driven ingestion. The migration paid off, but the in-between period — old system live, new system being trusted — required more babysitting than I'd planned.
text.absurd: Re-architected supply chain onboarding in Dagster: turned bespoke per-cluster orchestrations into a YAML-configured, data-contract-driven layer. This means onboarding a new supply chain went from "a quarter" to "an afternoon and a moderately serious lunch."

text.honest: Built monitoring for data quality, ingestion accuracy and frequency across all supply chain feeds.
text.pessimistic: Built monitoring for data quality, ingestion accuracy and frequency across feeds. Coverage is good now; getting the alerting thresholds calibrated took several false-positive cycles I'd run differently next time.
text.absurd: Built monitoring for data quality, ingestion accuracy, and frequency across every feed. The dashboards now know when they are sad before I do, which is a relationship dynamic I was not expecting from a piece of software.

text.honest: Earlier scope at OpenSC: an internal Android app (Kotlin), document scanning, internal tooling, API design, and orchestration in Airflow.
text.pessimistic: Earlier scope: internal Android app in Kotlin, document scanning, internal tooling, API design, Airflow orchestration. A useful range; not all of it was deep, and a couple of those early Airflow DAGs are still where I'd point if someone asked me to find a piece of code I'd want to rewrite.
text.absurd: Earlier scope at OpenSC: an internal Android app in Kotlin, document scanning, internal tooling, API design, and Airflow orchestration — a sampler platter of engineering, basically, served on a slowly improving plate.

summary.honest: UK investment consultancy. Built and led the frontend on a multi-tenant research platform used by several thousand consultants across 7+ tenants to track investments and maintain observability across portfolios. Joined as a junior; promoted to leading a frontend team within two years as the company scaled from 10 engineers to 8 teams.
summary.pessimistic: UK investment consultancy. Built and helped lead the frontend on a multi-tenant research platform — sensitive data, real consultants, real money. Joined as a junior, promoted into team lead within two years, which felt about a year earlier than I'd have promoted myself.
summary.absurd: UK investment consultancy. I joined as a junior and was promoted to leading a frontend team within two years, which is the corporate equivalent of being asked to perform open-heart surgery because you once watched the procedure on a long-haul flight. Several thousand consultants across 7+ tenants used this thing. Several thousand.

text.honest: Owned frontend architectural decisions on a React and TypeScript dashboard handling sensitive multi-tenant data, where cross-tenant leakage would have been existential to the business.
text.pessimistic: Owned frontend architectural decisions on a React and TypeScript dashboard handling sensitive multi-tenant data — the kind where a single cross-tenant leak would have been existential. Most of those decisions held up; a couple I'd unwind given another shot at them, particularly around how we handled tenant-scoped state in the early shared components.
text.absurd: Owned frontend architectural decisions on a React and TypeScript dashboard where cross-tenant data leakage would have been existential to the business — a sentence I would type onto my CV with one hand while a small voice inside me whispered "please, no." None of it leaked. None of it.

text.honest: Led a team of 5 engineers focused on frontend feature delivery; consistently the highest-performing team on UI work.
text.pessimistic: Led 5 engineers on frontend feature delivery; we were the highest-performing team on UI work, which sounds tidy but lived alongside the usual difficult trade-offs about who got the interesting tickets and who picked up the maintenance.
text.absurd: Led a team of 5 engineers focused on frontend feature delivery. Consistently the highest-performing team on UI work, which is the kind of compliment you put on a CV and pretend was just a normal Tuesday outcome.

text.honest: Co-led architectural decisions across the frontend codebase and implemented a significant portion of the dynamically configurable dashboard system.
text.pessimistic: Co-led architectural decisions and built a significant share of the dynamically configurable dashboard system. The flexibility we built was real; the cost was a configuration surface that newer engineers found genuinely hard to reason about.
text.absurd: Co-led architectural decisions across the frontend codebase and personally implemented a significant portion of the dynamically configurable dashboard system, the operative word being "dynamically." There are still meetings somewhere in which someone is trying to configure it.

text.honest: Drove introduction of E2E testing with Cypress, improving deployment confidence and reducing release-time human error.
text.pessimistic: Drove introduction of Cypress E2E testing. Deployment confidence improved; the suite also got slow enough at one point that engineers started mentally discounting failures, which is the failure mode that exists right next to "we have tests now."
text.absurd: Drove introduction of E2E testing with Cypress, improving deployment confidence and reducing release-time human error — a polite way of saying I tried to make it harder for any of us to accidentally show pension data to the wrong investment consultant on a Friday afternoon.

text.honest: Beyond engineering: chaired the Bristol charity committee. Ran a pro-bono engagement with LifeCycle — a charity left dry by multiple high-cost agencies — running a one-day discovery workshop and orchestrating the migration of their database and supporting infrastructure onto Airtable. Successfully pushed for an expansion of company-sponsored charity days and helped colleagues find opportunities.
text.pessimistic: Outside core engineering: chaired the Bristol charity committee, ran a pro-bono engagement with LifeCycle to migrate their infrastructure onto Airtable. Useful work; the discovery workshop was rougher than I'd run it now, and Airtable has limits I hadn't fully internalised when I picked it.
text.absurd: Outside engineering: chaired the Bristol charity committee, ran a pro-bono engagement for LifeCycle that involved migrating their entire database to Airtable in approximately one weekend and a strong cup of tea. Successfully lobbied for more charity days, which I consider, on balance, my most morally defensible commit.

notes: Developed the critical thinking, rhetoric, and ability to sit with difficult and ambiguous problems that still shapes how I approach engineering today

ai: Daily user of Claude Code and Cursor for the last 3+ months
Building 3 production-leaning side projects with LLMs (see Projects)
Developing a custom Claude Code setup with agents, skills, and telemetry
Maintaining a learnings catalogue via a session retrospective workflow
Currently training in: OWASP AI Top 10, prompt injection and jailbreaks, agent orchestration patterns, harness design for agentic systems
infra: AWS (S3, EKS, EC2, SNS, SQS, Lambda, IAM, RDS, CloudWatch)
leadership: Led a 5-person frontend team at Redington
Completed external senior-IC / principal-engineer leadership training

projects.description: Custom agents, skills, and telemetry layer over Claude CLI. Retrospective-driven learning catalogue captured after every working session.

avocations: Food, cooking, and the conversations around both
